title: Writing Files 📝
description: Create new files and permanently save your Ruby data directly to the hard drive.

slides.title: Write Mode ✍️
slides.content:
To write data to a file, you open it using the strict write mode: `"w"`.

Ruby handles the heavy lifting perfectly: If the file exists, Ruby opens it. If the file does NOT exist, Ruby instantly automatically creates a brand new blank file for you!

slides.title: The Danger of "w" ☢️
slides.content:
You must be incredibly careful when using write mode `"w"`.

When you open an existing file in `"w"` mode, Ruby instantly violently deletes every single line of data currently inside the file to give you a perfectly blank canvas!

It is incredibly destructive!

slides.title: Writing Data
slides.content: Run this! We safely open a file in write mode using a block, and then use the `file.puts` command to inject data gracefully directly into the hard drive.
slides.codeSnippet:
# Ruby literally creates 'my_notes.txt' on your computer
File.open("my_notes.txt", "w") do |file|
  file.puts "Ruby is so much fun!"
  file.puts "This text is now permanently saved."
end

puts "File successfully created and saved!"

quizData.question: What happens if you use `File.open("log.txt", "w")` on a file that already has 50 lines of incredibly important data saved in it?

options.text: Ruby adds your new text perfectly to line 51.
Ruby violently instantly erases all 50 lines, leaving the file completely blank for your new input.

quizData.explanation: "w" mode means explicitly "overwrite completely". It starts fresh every single time.

slides.title: Append Mode ➕
slides.content:
If you just want to neatly add a single new line of data to the very bottom of an existing file (like adding to a Log), you use Append mode: `"a"`.

Append mode elegantly perfectly preserves all existing data and smoothly adds your new text safely to the end.

slides.title: Appending Data
slides.content: Notice the `"a"` parameter. If this file existed, we are safely just adding a line to the bottom without destroying the history.
slides.codeSnippet:
File.open("log.txt", "a") do |file|
  file.puts "--- User logged in at 5:00 PM ---"
end

quizData.question: Which specific mode parameter strictly firmly tells Ruby you want perfectly safe addition access to the end of a file without destroying data?
quizData.explanation: Standard "a" stands specifically for 'Append', preventing you from accidentally beautifully deleting your data.
